summary: Gruber makes another case for switching from Google to Kagi for web search, citing a Boston Globe article by Aaron Pressman who was burned twice by Google's results — once overpaying for a UK travel authorization and once for hotel rooms — because semi-scam sites ranked above official ones. Gruber argues Kagi's superiority isn't about privacy or anti-Google protest but simply better search results, finding the right answer first more reliably than Google or DuckDuckGo. He notes that even Google's 'disenshittified' udm=14 mode produces worse results than Kagi, and that Kagi is especially better at finding older content. He compares paying for Kagi to paying for HBO during cable's heyday — you get both ad-free service and noticeably higher quality, except Kagi is a full replacement rather than a supplement.
key_points: Google's search results now routinely rank semi-scam sites and sponsored links above official government and business pages, costing users real money
Kagi consistently returns the correct, official result first where Google buries it below ads, AI overviews, and SEO-gamed sites
Gruber's case for Kagi is purely about result quality — not privacy, not protest, not ad avoidance — though those are bonuses
Unlike DuckDuckGo, where Gruber needed to redirect to Google once or twice a month, he effectively never needs to fall back from Kagi to Google
The HBO analogy: paying for Kagi gets you both ad-free experience and higher quality, and unlike HBO it's a full replacement not a supplement
money_quotes: I recommend switching to Kagi not because it's more private, not as a protest against Google, not as a rejection of search ads dominating the top of Google's results, but simply because Kagi's results are clearly better.
A complete scam would be taking your money and giving you nothing in return. These semi-scams deliver the thing they're promising, but charge you more than you should pay.
Decades of relying on Google had taught me that I didn't have to be a more careful consumer.
For me, Google and, alas, DuckDuckGo too have largely stopped working reliably for finding not-recent stuff on the web. Not true with Kagi.

Paying for Kagi today feels a lot like paying for HBO back in the cable TV heyday. Part of the deal is that you are paying for ad-free service, yes. But you're also paying for noticeably higher quality.
When you switch from Google to Kagi for search, you more or less switch. It's a full-on replacement.
I effectively never find myself redirecting the same search to Google because I wasn't happy with the results from Kagi.
key_insight: The case for Kagi over Google isn't ideological — it's purely practical: Kagi consistently returns the right result first, making it a straightforwardly better search engine worth paying for.
